content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Written in July 2026, somewhere between a Cursor tab and a Claude Code terminal.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

There’s a thing that happens at dinner parties now. Someone finds out I write software and says “oh, but doesn’t AI do all that now?” And someone else, usually a developer, visibly winces and starts explaining that real engineers don’t vibe code. Both of them are wrong, and both of them are wrong in the same way: they think vibe coding is one thing.
It isn’t. Vibe coding is a spectrum, and pretending it’s a single activity is like pretending “driving” means the same thing whether you’re in a go-kart or asleep in the back of a Waymo.
That comparison is not an accident. It’s the whole point of this article.
Borrowing the ladder from self-driving cars
The car industry solved this naming problem years ago. When people argued about whether a car “drives itself,” engineers got tired of the ambiguity and defined levels of autonomy, from Level 0 (you do everything) to Level 5 (there’s no steering wheel because why would there be).
Nobody argues about whether a Tesla and a Waymo are “the same thing” anymore. They just say the level. It’s precise, it’s boring, and it works.
Coding with AI deserves the same ladder. So here’s mine.
Level 0: Manual coding
You, a keyboard, and your own two hands. Maybe Stack Overflow if you’re feeling social.
This is not extinct and it’s not going extinct. When you’re writing the cryptographic core of something, or debugging a race condition at 2am, or working in a codebase where one wrong line costs real money or affects real patients, you want your hands on the wheel and your eyes on the road. Autocomplete off. Full attention.
The dinner party guest is wrong that this is dead. The wincing developer is wrong that this is the only respectable way to travel.
Level 1 to 2: Assisted coding
This is Copilot suggesting the next line. This is Cursor’s tab completion finishing the function you clearly started writing. The AI is lane assist. It keeps you centred, it saves you keystrokes, and every single character still passes through your judgement before it ships.
You review everything. You reject half of it. You are unambiguously the author, and if the code is bad, that’s on you the same way a fender bender is on you even if lane assist was on.
Most professional developers today live here and don’t even think of it as vibe coding. That’s fine. Lane assist drivers don’t think of themselves as passengers either.
Level 3: Supervised vibe coding
Here’s where it gets interesting, and here’s where I spend most of my working day.
Level 3 is agent mode in tools like Cursor. You describe what you want in plain language, the agent plans, edits multiple files, runs the tests, fixes its own mistakes, and presents you the result. You are not typing the code. But you are absolutely reading the code. Every diff. Every file. You have granular oversight, and the tool is built around giving you that oversight: reviewable diffs, checkpoints, the ability to reject a change per file, per hunk, per line.
The car is driving, but you’re in the driver’s seat, hands hovering, ready to take over. And crucially, you’re capable of taking over. Level 3 assumes a competent driver.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

This is the level the wincing developer should learn to respect, because the output here can be excellent. Not despite the human, but because of the human. The AI proposes, you dispose. It’s code review as a way of life.
Level 4: Delegated vibe coding
Claude Code. Background agents. You open a terminal, describe a task, and walk away. The agent works for twenty minutes, or two hours, across dozens of files, and comes back with a pull request.
You’re not watching it drive anymore. You’re reviewing the trip afterwards. You check the destination is right, you glance at the route, you look for anything alarming, and you merge. The review is real but it’s at the level of the pull request, not the keystroke.
This is where the trust question gets serious. At Level 3, a bad suggestion costs you a rejected diff. At Level 4, insufficient review costs you a subtle bug that shipped because it looked plausible and the tests happened to pass. Level 4 is safe roughly in proportion to how good your tests, your CI, and your reviewing discipline are. Delegation without verification isn’t a workflow, it’s a prayer.
Level 5: No-code, or full autonomy
Lovable, Bolt, v0, Replit’s agent. You describe an app, an app appears, and you may never look at the code at all. There is no steering wheel. There isn’t even a window into the engine bay.
And you know what? For a landing page, an internal tool, a weekend prototype, this is genuinely magical. The dinner party guest is describing Level 5 and thinks it’s all five levels. It isn’t. But it’s real, it works, and it’s put software creation in the hands of people who could never have built anything before. That matters.
Just don’t run your bank on it. Yet.
The part that’s actually changing
Here’s the observation I really wanted to write this article for.
The levels aren’t static. What’s moving, and moving fast, is where on the ladder we’re comfortable standing for any given task.
In 2023, Level 2 was the frontier for serious work. In 2024, Level 3 became normal. Through 2025 and into 2026, with models like Claude’s Fable generation and GPT-5-class agents, Level 4 stopped being a party trick and became a daily tool. Tasks I would have hand-written two years ago, I now delegate without a second thought. Tasks I would have supervised line by line last year, I now review at the PR level.
The models are earning their way down the stack. Not by marketing, but by repeatedly not screwing up. Trust in software has always worked like this. Nobody reads the assembly their compiler produces anymore, but in the 1950s, programmers absolutely did, because compilers were new and suspicious. Compilers earned blind trust over decades. AI coding agents are speedrunning the same journey.
So what level should you be on?
Wrong question. The right question is: what level does this task deserve?
I move up and down the ladder a dozen times a day. Migration script for throwaway data? Level 4, go, don’t care. New feature in the payment path? Level 3, and I read every line twice. Weird production bug in code I barely understand? Level 0 to 1, hands on the wheel, radio off. Mockup to show a stakeholder tomorrow morning? Level 5, generate me something pretty.
The skill of a modern developer is no longer just writing code. It’s calibration. Knowing what to delegate, what to supervise, and what to do yourself. The engineers who struggle right now are the ones stuck at a single level: the purist who won’t go above 1 and gets outpaced, and the maximalist who won’t go below 4 and ships plausible-looking bugs at incredible velocity.
All vibe coding is equal
Except it isn’t, and now you have the words to say why.
Next time someone lumps together a founder prompting Lovable, a developer reviewing Cursor diffs, and an engineer delegating to Claude Code, you can tell them those are three different levels of a five-level ladder, with different trust models, different failure modes, and different skills.
And next time someone tells you real engineers don’t vibe code, ask them if they read the assembly their compiler produced this morning.
Some pigs, as it turns out, are just further down the autonomy stack than others.
